title: Humility Leads to Community
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: Humility Leads to Community - Pastor Tito Lyro - Ephesians 4_1-6.txt

outline.title: Introduction: Humility Leads to Community
subpoints: This is the first of four sermons on humility as the theme for 2022 — a four-week break from the 1 John series
Christianity is always relational; the gospel must be lived in community; pride kills relationships
Every relational problem — with God and with each other — has pride as its root, in our own hearts or the hearts of those around us
Life in community, life in unity, flows out of a humble heart

outline.title: 1. Life in the Gospel Can Be Lived Under Any Circumstances
subpoints: Paul writes from prison: 'I, the prisoner of the Lord, beseech you to walk worthy of the calling' — he does not use his chains to manipulate but demonstrates that life in the gospel is possible under any circumstances
He describes himself as 'a prisoner in the Lord' (literally in the Greek) — his faith in Christ pervasively shapes how he carries himself even as a prisoner
We do not have to wait for circumstances to align — not for our spouse to change, children to grow, or finances to improve — in order to live out who we are in Christ
James Boice: 'God who has awakened us to a new life also gives us the power to live that life'

outline.title: 2. The Virtues That Build Community
subpoints: Verses 2-3: 'With all lowliness and gentleness, with longsuffering, bearing with one another in love, endeavoring to keep the unity of the Spirit in the bond of peace'
These two verses are the operating system of everything that follows in Ephesians — the rest of the book describes this walk, and this walk is always relational
Humility, gentleness, patience, love, and peace are the fruit of the Spirit — Paul is saying the fruit of the Spirit must pervade the way we live in all relationships
The bond of unity in the church is not sameness but love — diversity in unity; we need each other precisely because we are different

outline.title: 3. Unity Is Spirit-Born; Discord Is the Work of Satan
subpoints: A church in disarray gives testimony that the Spirit of God is not present — Romans 8:6: 'To be carnally minded is death, but to be spiritually minded is life and peace'
When we sow disunity through gossip, slander, pride, self-centeredness, laziness, or an unforgiving spirit, we are instruments of Satan in his attempt to destroy the church
Paul is severe with the contentious person — Titus 3:10-11: 'Reject a divisive man after the first and second admonition, knowing that such a person is warped and sinning, being self-condemned'
Unity demonstrates who our God is; when we live in humble unity, we show who the one God, one Spirit, one Lord is (vv.4-6)

outline.title: 4. What Humility Is and Is Not
subpoints: Humility is not self-deprecation in hope of being exalted by others — that is a subtle form of pride
C.S. Lewis: a truly humble man 'will not be thinking about humility. He will not be thinking about himself at all' — he will seem a cheerful person who takes a real interest in what you say
Humility is thinking of others rather than oneself; it is esteeming others better than yourself (Philippians 2:3-4); preferring others
Proverbs 27:2: 'Let another man praise you, and not your own mouth' — those who have to say they are humble likely are not

outline.title: 5. Pride Must Die — What the Bible Says about Pride
subpoints: Pride is a fruit of the flesh (James 4:6); pride is Satan's chief sin; pride results in shame (Proverbs 11:2); pride brings destruction (Proverbs 16:18)
God hates pride and will destroy the house of the proud; the proud does not trust in the Lord — pride is the ultimate act of unbelief
Knowing the depths from which we have been raised — dead in trespasses — and the heights to which we have been brought — sons and daughters of God — must produce humility

Hodge: 'Where these effects [humility] are not found, we may conclude the exaltation has not taken place' — if we are not humble, we likely do not know the depth of our sin or the height of God's grace

summary:
Opening the year's four-part series on humility, Pastor Tito Lyro preaches from Ephesians 4:1-6 on the connection between humility and community. Christianity, he argues, is always relational — it cannot be lived in isolation — and every relational problem, whether with God or with others, has pride as its root. Life in community requires humility as its engine. The gospel transforms relationships precisely because it humbles the person who truly grasps its content.

Paul writes Ephesians 4 from prison, which itself demonstrates that the gospel life can be lived under any circumstances. Verses 2-3 function as the operating system for everything that follows in the letter: all loneliness, gentleness, longsuffering, bearing with one another in love, keeping the unity of the Spirit in the bond of peace. These virtues are not incidental — they are the fruit of the Spirit. The bond of the church's unity is not sameness but love, and God equips the church with diverse gifts precisely so that its members need one another.

Discord in the church is not a neutral circumstance — it is evidence that the Spirit is not present and that we are being used as instruments of Satan. Conversely, Charles Hodge observes that knowing the depth from which we have been raised (dead in sin) and the height to which we have been brought (sons of God) must produce humility. If it does not, we likely do not truly understand what God has done. The sermon closes with C.S. Lewis's portrait of the genuinely humble person — not someone constantly advertising his humility, but someone who simply is not thinking about himself at all, fully absorbed in others.
transcript:
## Introduction: Humility Leads to Community

We are taking a four-week break from our 1 John series to talk about a theme for 2022. The theme I have been presenting to you — this is the first Sunday we will be talking about it — is humility. And today I want to suggest that humility leads to community.

Faith in the Lord Jesus Christ always translates itself into a pattern of living. And this pattern of living is always relational. Christianity cannot be lived in isolation. Christianity is always lived in community, in relationships. The gospel is always lived out in community. And we cannot live in community if we are full of pride. Pride kills relationships.

I am thoroughly convinced that every relational problem we have — both in our relationship with God and with each other — has, as its root, pride in our own hearts or in the hearts of those around us. So life in community, life in unity as Paul talks about in Ephesians 4, flows out of a humble heart. That is why we are striving for humility.

As we look forward to this new year — and it is a new year today, it is only the ninth of January; most resolutions are not broken yet, we have a couple weeks still — may we pursue Christian humility so that we may live in unity with one another for the glory of God.

Ephesians 4, verses 1-6:

*I, therefore, the prisoner of the Lord, beseech you to walk worthy of the calling with which you were called, with all lowliness and gentleness, with longsuffering, bearing with one another in love, endeavoring to keep the unity of the Spirit in the bond of peace. There is one body and one Spirit, just as you were called in one hope of your calling; one Lord, one faith, one baptism; one God and Father of all, who is above all, and through all, and in you all.*

## 1. Life Can Be Lived in the Light of the Gospel Under Any Circumstances

Have you ever thought: if these things fall into place, then I can be obedient to Christ? If this happens, then I can grow in humility? Paul teaches us here that that is not the case — that life can be lived in the light of the gospel under any circumstances.

You see that in verse 1. He says: 'I, the prisoner of the Lord, beseech you to walk worthy of the calling with which you were called.' Because of all that I have taught you in chapters 1 through 3, I want you to live in a certain way.

Notice how Paul brings out the fact that he is in prison. He is not trying to manipulate the Ephesians — 'Look, I am in prison, so go ahead and do it.' The word translated 'of' in 'prisoner of the Lord' in most translations is literally 'in' in the Greek: 'I am a prisoner in the Lord.' The way he is supposed to live at that moment — his calling — is as a prisoner. I am behaving right now as a prisoner in the Lord. The work of God through Christ in his life dominates the way he is as a prisoner.

By referring to himself as a prisoner in the Lord, Paul demonstrates that living in the light of the gospel is something the Christian does under any circumstances — including living in humility. The stars do not have to align a certain way. Things do not have to be favorable for us to live in the light of the gospel. Even in prison, even facing a potential death penalty, one can live in the light of the gospel.

Brothers and sisters, we do not have to wait for a particular set of circumstances to live out who we are in Jesus Christ. We do not have to wait for our spouse to change. We do not have to wait for our children to grow up. We do not have to wait until we make more money. We do it now. James Boice says: 'God who has awakened us to a new life also gives us the power to live that life.' You already have the Spirit of God in you in whatever circumstances you are in — and you can grow in humility now.

## 2. The Virtues That Build Community

In verses 2 and 3, Paul says: 'With all lowliness and gentleness, with longsuffering, bearing with one another in love, endeavoring to keep the unity of the Spirit in the bond of peace.'

What Paul says here controls all that follows in the book of Ephesians. These two verses are the operating system of whatever else he says in the rest of the letter. In essence, the rest of the book describes this walk — and this walk is always relational. This life that the Christian lives is always in relationship.

So humility, gentleness, patience, love, and peace will mark all the relationships described in the rest of the book. Husbands and wives. Parents and children. Employees and employers. All of it. And what does that remind you of? Those are the fruit of the Spirit. Paul is saying that the fruit of the Spirit is to pervade and mark the way that we live in life-in-community.

Life in unity — life equivalent to our status as children of God — is marked by unity, not discord, not disunity, not fighting, but unity. We are united together not because we are the same, but because we love each other. Diversity in unity. Unity in diversity. There is a great diversity of gifts in the church, all of them needed for the unity of the body. The reason we can be united is precisely because we are different from one another. God is not calling us to be copies of one another — he is calling us to be copies of Christ. He equips us so that we need each other.

## 3. Unity Is Spirit-Born; Discord Is Satan's Work

This unity is Spirit-born — it is evidence of the work of the Spirit in the life of the church. A church that is in disarray is giving testimony that the Spirit of God is not present in that church. Romans 8:6: 'For to be carnally minded is death, but to be spiritually minded is life and peace.' When there is a lack of peace, there is also evidence of a carnal mind present.

Disunity, disarray — present in a congregation — is not the work of the Holy Spirit. It is the work of the prince of this world. When we sow disunity through gossip, through slander, through pride, through self-centeredness, through laziness, through an unforgiving spirit, we are instruments of Satan in his attempt to destroy the church.

I want you to think about your life and where there is disarray. Where is there self-centeredness? Laziness? Arrogance? Which of your relationships are broken because your heart is unwilling to forgive those around you? We cannot forgive until we are asked for forgiveness — but we can have a heart ready and able to forgive. If you do not have that, you will have a collection of broken relationships.

That is why Paul is so hard on the contentious person in Titus 3:10-11: 'Reject a divisive man after the first and second admonition, knowing that such a person is warped and sinning, being self-condemned.' What marks your life? Is it confusion and division, or unity? Your relationships demonstrate the oneness of our God and our faith.

When we live in humble unity with one another, we are demonstrating who our God is. Paul says that in verses 4-6: 'There is one body and one Spirit, just as you were called in one hope of your calling; one Lord, one faith, one baptism; one God and Father of all.' The one God, one Spirit, one Lord has called us into one body. All these realities encourage us to live out our faith in Christ in unity with the brethren.

## 4. Humility — The Engine of Community

And look at verse 2 again — that first word, 'lowliness,' is humility. That is the driving force. The engine of the train of relationships. This is what will move us toward unity in the church — humility in the church of the Lord Jesus Christ.

Charles Hodge, the well-known Princeton professor of the 1800s, said this concerning this passage: 'To be raised from the depths of degradation and misery and made the sons of God — and thus exalted to an inconceivable elevation of dignity — does and must produce humility and meekness. Where these effects are not found, we may conclude the exaltation has not taken place.'

Do you see what he is saying? We are in the depths of degradation, in the depths of misery. That is every one of us — not somebody else, not the really bad people out there, but every descendant of Adam and Eve. For God to reach down into that miry pit, raise us up, set us on a rock, give us a new song, and call us sons and daughters of himself through Jesus Christ — knowing where we came from, and knowing the height to which we have been brought — that has to produce humility. If it does not, we likely do not know the depth of what we were or the height of what God has done.

## 5. What Pride Does and What Humility Is

Pride will invariably lead to conflict and disunity. At the root of every conflict there is pride somewhere. John Calvin asks: where does rudeness come from? Where does pride and disdainful language toward the brethren come from? Where do quarrels, insults, and reproaches come from? They come from this: everyone carries his love of himself to excess. That is the source.

Now listen to what the Bible says concerning pride. Pride is a fruit of the flesh (James 4:6). Pride keeps you from listening to God's Word and destroys relationships (1 Timothy 6:3-5). Pride comes from an evil heart — Jesus says so explicitly in Mark 7:22. Pride is Satan's chief sin. The qualification for an elder in 1 Timothy 3 warns that a novice in the faith may fall into the same sin of pride as Satan. Pride is the result of exchanging the truth of God for a lie (Romans 1:30). God resists the proud (1 Peter 5:5). Pride results in shame (Proverbs 11:2). Pride is characteristic of the fool — Proverbs 14:3: 'In the mouth of a fool is a rod of pride,' which he uses to beat other people. Pride brings destruction (Proverbs 16:18). Stay away from the proud (Proverbs 16:19). Pride is sin (Proverbs 21:4). Pride is the ultimate act of unbelief — Proverbs 28:25 contrasts the proud heart with the one who trusts in the Lord.

So as children of God, we understand there is no reason for us to be proud. Pride dies and is replaced with humility. Philippians 2:3: 'Let nothing be done through selfish ambition or conceit, but in lowliness of mind let each esteem others better than himself. Let each of you look out not only for his own interests, but also for the interests of others.'

In ancient Greek literature, the word humility was a cussword — used only to criticize people. If you were not exalting yourself, something was wrong with you. Humility is not a characteristic of the world; it is essential for a spiritual life in community. It goes against human nature. Yet our Savior himself is our example of humility as he became a man and humbled himself — Philippians 2:7-8: 'He humbled himself and became obedient to the point of death, even the death of the cross.'

Now — what is humility? Humility is not saying you are worse than everybody else. Self-deprecation is not necessarily humility. Often we deprecate ourselves hoping that someone will exalt us — 'I am really bad at this.' 'Oh no, you're great!' 'Of course I am — I just wanted you to say it.' That is self-deprecation, not humility.

If you are good at something, denying it does not make you humble. Humility includes acknowledging the gifts God has given you and using them in his service by serving others. Humility is really not thinking about yourself.

Proverbs 27:2: 'Let another man praise you, and not your own mouth; a stranger, and not your own lips.'

C.S. Lewis says: 'Do not imagine that if you meet a really humble man you will find what most people call humble nowadays. He will not be a sort of greasy, smarmy person who is always telling you that of course he is nobody. Probably all you will think about him is that he seemed a cheerful intelligent chap who took a real interest in what you said to him. He will not be thinking about humility. He will not be thinking about himself at all.'

That is true. And in the musical *Hamilton*, there is an interesting exchange of letters between Alexander Hamilton and Aaron Burr, where they criticize each other back and forth — and always end with 'Your humble servant, A.H.' or 'A. Burr.' If you find yourself having to say that you are humble, you are probably not.

Your concern is to die to self, to think of others as more important than yourself, to prefer and serve them in love.

## Conclusion: Kill Pride Before Pride Kills You

Life in community, life in unity, flows out of humble hearts. As we look forward to this new year, may we pursue Christian humility so that we may live in unity with one another for the glory of God.

Kill pride before pride kills you. Humble yourself before the Lord and let him exalt you. And sometimes he exalts through suffering — he exalted his Son through the cross. But he will exalt you. He promises that if not in this life, then in the life to come — he will raise you from the dead and bring you back to life. And forever you will be with Jesus Christ, and you will be like him. That is glory to God.

Father, thank you that you are a God who loves the humble. Thank you that your Spirit dwells in us and that you have already limited the reigning power of sin over us. Enable us to put off pride — that is consistent with who we were apart from Christ — and put on humility, which is the mind of our Savior. Kill pride in our lives. Help us live in humility, and may that humility generate unity in your church. We praise you, thanking you in Jesus' name. Amen.
